source_paper_title: Fair Shares: Feasibility, Domination, and Incentives
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Babaioff_2025, title={Fair Shares: Feasibility, Domination, and Incentives}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0257}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0257}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Babaioff, Moshe and Feige, Uriel}, year={2025}, month=aug, pages={1901–1934} }
context:
Page 7, Section 1.2 (Our Results), end of paragraph discussing [GT20] and Theorem 3; reiterated Page 26, Section 7 (Discussion), final paragraph.

We leave open the problem of finding the largest value of \(\rho\) for which there is a \(\rho\)-dominating share that is feasible, self maximizing, and poly-time computable for additive valuations.

Finally, we note that we leave open the problem of finding (for additive valuations, and also beyond this class), the largest value of \(\rho\) for which there exists a feasible share that is \(\rho\)-dominating, self maximizing and poly-time computable.
title: Largest achievable domination factor for feasible self-maximizing polynomial-time shares under additivity
background_latex:
Consider fair division of a finite set of indivisible goods \(M\) among \(n\ge 2\) equally entitled agents, with no monetary transfers. Each agent has an additive valuation \(v:2^M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), meaning there are item values \(v(j)\ge 0\) for \(j\in M\) and \(v(S)=\sum_{j\in S} v(j)\) for all \(S\subseteq M\). An allocation is a partition \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) of \(M\) into \(n\) (possibly empty) disjoint bundles.

A (fairness) share is a function \(s\) mapping a valuation \(v\) and an agent count \(n\) to a nonnegative value \(s(v,n)\). A share \(s\) is feasible (for additive valuations) if for every instance \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\) of additive valuations there exists an allocation \(A\) such that \(v_i(A_i)\ge s(v_i,n)\) for all agents \(i\).

Given a share \(s\), define its (worst acceptable-bundle) guarantee \(\hat s(v,n)\) as the minimum value (under \(v\)) among bundles whose value is at least \(s(v,n)\):
\[\hat s(v,n)=\min\{v(S): S\subseteq M,\ v(S)\ge s(v,n)\}.\]
For a true valuation \(v\) and a reported valuation \(v'\), define the implied guarantee
\[\hat s_v(v') = \min\{v(S): S\subseteq M,\ v'(S)\ge s(v',n)\}.\]
The share \(s\) is self-maximizing if truthful reporting maximizes the implied guarantee for every additive \(v\): \(\hat s_v(v)\ge \hat s_v(v')\) for all reports \(v'\).

The maximin share (MMS) for additive \(v\) and \(n\) agents is
\[\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)=\max_{(B_1,\dots,B_n)\text{ partition of }M}\ \min_{j\in[n]} v(B_j).\]
A share \(s\) is \(\rho\)-dominating (for a fixed \(n\), or uniformly over \(n\)) if it guarantees at least a \(\rho\) fraction of the MMS pointwise:
\[ s(v,n)\ \ge\ \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\qquad \text{for all additive } v.\]
(Equivalently, it \(\rho\)-dominates every feasible share, since \(\mathrm{MMS}\) dominates all feasible shares when defined on a fixed \((M,n)\).)

Computability requirement: \(s\) is polynomial-time computable if, given additive item values (or value-query access), one can compute \(s(v,n)\) in time polynomial in \(|M|\) (and the input encoding).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine
\[\rho^* = \sup\Big\{\rho\in[0,1]: \exists\ \text{a share } s \text{ for additive valuations such that}\Big\}
\]
subject to all of the following holding (uniformly for all \(n\ge 2\)):

1) (Feasibility) For every \(n\ge 2\) and every additive instance \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\), there exists an allocation \(A\) with \(v_i(A_i)\ge s(v_i,n)\) for all \(i\).

2) (Self-maximizing) For every \(n\ge 2\), every additive true valuation \(v\), and every additive report \(v'\), \(\hat s_v(v)\ge \hat s_v(v')\).

3) (Polynomial-time computable) For every \(n\ge 2\) and additive \(v\), the value \(s(v,n)\) can be computed in polynomial time.

4) (\(\rho\)-domination of MMS) For every \(n\ge 2\) and additive \(v\), \(s(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\).

Equivalently, compute the maximal approximation factor \(\rho^*\) achievable by feasible, self-maximizing, polynomial-time computable shares relative to \(\mathrm{MMS}_n\) for additive valuations.

Each agent has an additive valuation \(v:2^M\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}\), meaning there are item values \(v(j)\ge 0\) for \(j\in M\) and \(v(S)=\sum_{j\in S} v(j)\) for all \(S\subseteq M\). An allocation is a partition \(A=(A_1,\dots,A_n)\) of \(M\) into \(n\) (possibly empty) disjoint bundles.

\[\hat s(v,n)=\min\{v(S): S\subseteq M,\ v(S)\ge s(v,n)\}.\]

Determine
\[\rho^* = \sup\Big\{\rho\in[0,1]: \exists\ \text{a share } s \text{ for additive valuations such that}\Big\}
\]
subject to all of the following holding (uniformly for all \(n\ge 2\)):

1) (Feasibility) For every \(n\ge 2\) and every additive instance \((v_1,\dots,v_n)\), there exists an allocation \(A\) with \(v_i(A_i)\ge s(v_i,n)\) for all \(i\).

2) (Self-maximizing) For every \(n\ge 2\), every additive true valuation \(v\), and every additive report \(v'\), \(\hat s_v(v)\ge \hat s_v(v')\).

location_in_paper: Page 7, Section 1.2 (Our Results), end of paragraph discussing [GT20] and Theorem 3; reiterated Page 26, Section 7 (Discussion), final paragraph.
open_problem_quote:
We leave open the problem of finding the largest value of \(\rho\) for which there is a \(\rho\)-dominating share that is feasible, self maximizing, and poly-time computable for additive valuations.

Finally, we note that we leave open the problem of finding (for additive valuations, and also beyond this class), the largest value of \(\rho\) for which there exists a feasible share that is \(\rho\)-dominating, self maximizing and poly-time computable.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over small additive instances to upper-bound achievable \(\rho\) for restricted families of self-maximizing feasible shares, but the full object being optimized ranges over all such share functions, which is not a finite search space. Still, automated instance generation can help refute candidate \(\rho\) values for proposed constructions by finding violations of feasibility or domination.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate shares can be represented by parameterized rules (e.g., ordinal/maximin constructions, picking-order-derived families, dynamic programs) with computable objectives like worst-case \(\min_v s(v,n)/\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\) over sampled instances and feasibility checks via allocation algorithms. However, verifying universal feasibility and proving tight bounds likely requires substantial theory beyond what an evolutionary loop can certify.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The Babaioff--Feige open problem asks for the largest uniform \(\rho^*\) such that there exists a share function \(s(v,n)\) for additive valuations that is simultaneously feasible (there is always an allocation meeting all agents' shares), self-maximizing in the implied-guarantee sense \(\hat s_v(v)\ge \hat s_v(v')\), polynomial-time computable, and pointwise \(\rho\)-dominating the maximin share \(\mathrm{MMS}_n\). In the source paper, Babaioff and Feige introduce the share/contract viewpoint, prove that exact SM-domination is impossible for any class containing additive valuations (via picking-order shares and MMS infeasibility), and then give explicit positive constructions: ordinal maximin shares (self-maximizing by design) and the nested shares \(\mathrm{NS}_{n,3}\), which are feasible, polynomial-time computable, and guarantee \(\frac{2n}{3n-1}\)-MMS for all \(n\), improving to \(4/5\)-MMS for \(n\le 4\); for \(n=2\) they give a PTAS achieving \((1-\varepsilon)\)-MMS.

Subsequent work in the forward-citation set does not determine \(\rho^*\) in this exact framework. Several papers explore adjacent notions of feasible shares: quantile shares and thinned quantile shares establish constant-quantile feasibility (even universal feasibility for all monotone valuations after thinning), and these benchmarks are also (informally) self-maximizing and efficiently approximable by sampling. However, these works do not provide pointwise lower bounds of the form \(s(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\) for all additive \(v\), so they do not improve the best known \(\rho\) under the MMS-domination requirement.

On the incentives side, work on truthful(-in-expectation) mechanisms and on characterizations of deterministic truthful mechanisms highlights strong constraints when one insists on dominant-strategy truthfulness of allocation rules; these results yield poor MMS approximations for broad classes of truthful mechanisms. While conceptually related, they do not translate directly to the implied-guarantee self-maximization condition for shares. Overall, the state of the art remains that constant \(\rho\) is achievable (at least \(\approx 2/3\) uniformly in \(n\) via nested shares, and \(4/5\) for \(n\le 4\)), but the optimal uniform \(\rho^*\) for feasible, self-maximizing, polynomial-time shares that dominate \(\mathrm{MMS}_n\) for additive valuations remains open.

sources.title: Fair division via quantile shares
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper introduces the q-quantile share \(\tau_q(v,n)\), defined as the \(q\)-quantile of \(v(X)\) where \(X\) is the random bundle obtained by assigning each item independently to an agent with probability \(1/n\). It proves feasibility results for this share notion: - (Conditional) If a special case of the Rainbow Erd\H{o}s Matching Conjecture holds, then the \(1/(2e)\)-quantile share is universally feasible for all monotone valuations (Theorem 2). - (Unconditional) For additive valuations, the \(q\)-quantile share is feasible for \(q<0.14/e\) asymptotically (Proposition 4). - For unit-demand and matroid-rank valuations, feasibility holds up to \(q\approx 1/e\) (Propositions 5–6), and \(q>1/e\) is infeasible for large \(n\) (via Proposition 3).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the largest \(\rho^*\) such that there exists a share \(s\) that is simultaneously (i) feasible for additive valuations, (ii) self-maximizing in the Babaioff–Feige sense (truthful report maximizes the implied worst acceptable-bundle guarantee \(\hat s_v\)), (iii) polynomial-time computable, and (iv) \(\rho\)-dominates MMS pointwise: \(s(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\) for all additive \(v\), uniformly over \(n\).

This paper studies a different share family (quantile shares) and establishes feasibility/universal-feasibility thresholds for \(\tau_q\). It notes that quantile shares are self-maximizing (in an informal sense) and efficiently approximable by sampling, but it does not analyze domination relative to MMS, nor does it optimize \(\rho\) in \(s(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\). In fact, quantile shares are not designed to approximate MMS and can be either more or less demanding than MMS fractions depending on the instance (Example 2). Therefore it does not resolve \(\rho^*\) and does not provide a candidate share with a proven constant \(\rho\)-MMS domination guarantee under the self-maximizing + feasibility + polytime constraints.

sources.title: Thinned Quantile Shares are Universally Feasible
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main results (for general monotone valuations):
- There exists a universal constant \(c>0\) such that the \(c\)-thinned \(e^{-c}\)-quantile share \(\tau^{(c)}_{e^{-c}}(v,n)\) is universally feasible (Theorem 1.5 / Theorem 4.2).
- Assuming the rainbow Erdős matching conjecture, the (unthinned) \((1/e)\)-quantile share is universally feasible (Theorem 1.7), and for any \(c\in(0,1]\) the \(c\)-thinned \(q\)-quantile share can be infeasible for any \(q>e^{-c}\) (Proposition 1.8).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the largest \(\rho^*\) such that there exists a share \(s\) for additive valuations that is simultaneously (i) feasible, (ii) self-maximizing in the Babaioff–Feige sense, (iii) polynomial-time computable, and (iv) \(\rho\)-dominates \(\mathrm{MMS}_n\) pointwise.

This paper studies a different share family (quantile shares and their \(c\)-thinned variant) and proves strong feasibility results—indeed universal feasibility for all monotone valuations—for \(\tau^{(c)}_{e^{-c}}\). Quantile shares are known to be self-maximizing (and the paper states this extends to thinned quantile shares), and they are Monte-Carlo approximable.

However, the paper does not analyze domination of \(\mathrm{MMS}\) for additive valuations, nor does it optimize or even provide a constant \(\rho\) such that \(\tau^{(c)}_{e^{-c}}(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\) for all additive \(v\). In fact, since Babichenko et al. show no constant fraction of MMS is universally feasible over all monotone valuations, these universally feasible quantile-type shares cannot \(\rho\)-dominate MMS uniformly over all monotone valuations; the open problem is restricted to additive valuations, but this paper does not bridge that gap.

Thus, the connection is mainly methodological/background: it provides a new universally feasible, self-maximizing benchmark share (thinned quantile share) and extremal-set-theory tools, but it does not resolve \(\rho^*\) for additive valuations under the additional MMS-domination requirement.

sources.title: Truthful-in-Expectation Mechanisms for MMS Approximation
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper designs polynomial-time randomized allocation mechanisms for strategic agents with additive valuations that are truthful-in-expectation (TIE) and guarantee ex-post approximations to MMS (and TPS). Main stated guarantees include: - For general n, an ordinal TIE distributional mechanism that is ex-ante proportional and guarantees each agent at least \(\frac{1}{H_{n-1}+2}\)-MMS ex-post (Theorem 1). - With small additional cardinal information and relaxing incentives to \((1-\varepsilon(n))\)-TIE (negligible \(\varepsilon(n)=n^{-\log n}\)), an ex-ante proportional mechanism guaranteeing \(\Omega(1/\log\log n)\)-MMS ex-post (Theorem 2). - For \(n=2\), a TIE distributional mechanism that is ex-ante proportional and \(\frac{2}{3}\)-MMS ex-post (Theorem 3).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the largest domination factor \(\rho^*\) achievable by a share function \(s(v,n)\) that is (i) feasible for all additive instances, (ii) self-maximizing in the sense that truthful reporting maximizes the implied guarantee \(\hat s_v(\cdot)\), (iii) polynomial-time computable, and (iv) pointwise \(\rho\)-dominating MMS: \(s(v,n)\ge \rho\,\mathrm{MMS}_n(v)\) for all additive \(v\), uniformly over \(n\).
This paper does not study shares \(s(v,n)\) with the feasibility/self-maximizing definition from Babaioff–Feige ("Fair Shares"). Instead it studies TIE mechanisms (randomized allocation rules) that approximate MMS/TPS ex-post. TIE is an incentive notion about expected utility, not the paper’s self-maximizing share notion based on worst acceptable bundles \(\hat s\). Likewise, the paper’s guarantees are about existence of a mechanism producing allocations with ex-post MMS approximation, not about a per-agent share function \(s(v,n)\) that is always simultaneously feasible across all agents’ shares.
Nevertheless, the paper is partially relevant: it provides new incentive-compatible (in expectation) constructions and impossibility insights around truthful fair division without payments, and it highlights computable benchmarks (TPS) and approximation factors (e.g., \(1/(H_{n-1}+2)\), \(\Omega(1/\log\log n)\), \(2/3\) for \(n=2\)) that could inform upper/lower bounds for \(\rho^*\) if one attempts to relate self-maximizing shares to implementable truthful mechanisms. But it does not directly bound or determine \(\rho^*\) under the self-maximizing-share framework.

sources.title: Near-Optimal Best-of-Both-Worlds Fairness for Few Agents
sources.theorem_or_result:
For additive valuations with a fixed small number of agents, the paper gives Best-of-Both-Worlds (BoBW) randomized allocation algorithms/distributions with strong ex-ante proportionality/envy-freeness and strong ex-post guarantees. In particular:
- For 2 agents and any \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an FPTAS outputting a distribution over at most two allocations that is ex-ante envy-free (hence proportional), ex-post EFX, and in every realized allocation each agent gets at least \((1-\varepsilon)\,\mathrm{MMS}_2(v_i)\) (Theorem 1.2).
- For 3 agents, there exists an ex-ante proportional distribution supported on at most six allocations such that every realized allocation is EEFX and gives each agent at least \(\tfrac{9}{10}\,\mathrm{MMS}_3(v_i)\), and moreover is IMMX (each agent either gets her MMS or is EFX-satisfied) (Theorem 1.1). Polynomial-time variants via FPTAS achieve ex-ante proportionality with ex-post \((\tfrac{9}{10}-\varepsilon)\)-MMS (Theorem 5.1) or approximate ex-ante proportionality with EEFX retained (Theorem 3.2).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for the maximal domination factor \(\rho^*\) achievable by shares \(s(v,n)\) that are simultaneously (i) feasible for all additive instances, (ii) self-maximizing (truthful reporting maximizes the implied guarantee \(\hat s_v\)), and (iii) polynomial-time computable, while (iv) pointwise \(\rho\)-dominating MMS for all \(n\ge2\).

This paper does not study shares \(s(v,n)\) or the self-maximizing (incentive) constraint; instead it studies randomized allocation algorithms achieving ex-ante/ex-post fairness (BoBW) for \(n=2,3\). Its results are therefore not direct progress on determining \(\rho^*\) under self-maximization.

Indirectly, the paper strengthens what is achievable (algorithmically, and in some cases with FPTAS) in terms of ex-post MMS approximation factors for small \(n\) while also satisfying strong envy-based properties (EFX/EEFX). These constructions might inspire candidate shares or feasibility constructions, but they do not establish any \(\rho\)-dominating self-maximizing polynomial-time share uniformly over all \(n\), nor upper/lower bounds on \(\rho^*\).

sources.title: On Truthful Mechanisms without Pareto-efficiency: Characterizations and Fairness
sources.theorem_or_result: Main characterization (Theorem 1 / Application 1): For any permutations-closed class of strict preferences (or strict valuations) that contains all lexicographic preferences—hence in particular any class containing additive valuations—every deterministic mechanism that is strategy-proof (truthful), non-bossy, and neutral (even without Pareto-efficiency and even allowing disposal) must be a serial-quota mechanism (fixed agent order and fixed quotas; each agent in turn picks her quota-demand from remaining goods). Consequently, for additive valuations, any such truthful mechanism has very poor MMS approximation, at most \(1/\lfloor (m-n+2)/2\rfloor\) (Application 2, via known bounds for serial-quota mechanisms).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for the best domination factor \(\rho^*\) achievable by feasible, self-maximizing, polynomial-time computable shares relative to MMS under additivity. This paper does not study shares, feasibility of shares, or the self-maximizing condition \(\hat s_v(v)\ge \hat s_v(v')\). Instead it characterizes truthful (strategy-proof) allocation mechanisms under neutrality and non-bossiness, and derives impossibility/upper bounds on MMS approximation for such mechanisms. While conceptually related (both restrict incentives and compare to MMS), the incentive notion here (dominant-strategy truthfulness of mechanisms) is different from self-maximizing shares, so the paper does not resolve \(\rho^*\). It may provide techniques/negative intuition suggesting strong limits when incentives are imposed, but it does not translate directly to the share framework.
